behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: The tool must invoke git, parse its rename output format (e.g., `R100 old_file new_file` from `git diff --name-status`), and categorize it correctly. A unit test with mocked git output could miss bugs in the git invocation itself or subtle parsing errors in handling rename status codes. A real git repository with an actual rename is the boundary where this behavior can fail — if the code treats renames as deletion+addition instead, only a real git repo will expose it.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test git repository, commit a file, rename it using `git mv`, run the tool on this repository, and verify that the output contains a 'Renamed' category with exactly one file, and that the file does not appear in 'Added' or 'Deleted' categories.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Binary file handling in git output is specific (e.g., `Binary files a/file and b/file differ`). A mocked git output might not capture all edge cases of how git encodes binary differences, and the code might crash on real binary diff output in ways that mocks won't reveal. The tool must correctly parse and count binary file entries from actual git output without crashing. Only a real repository with actual binary files and git's real diff output will reliably catch crashes or miscounting.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test git repository with a binary file (e.g., a PNG or PDF), add or modify it, run the tool, and verify that (1) the tool exits successfully without crashing and (2) the binary file is correctly counted in the output report.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: Git's behavior on a fresh repository (no commits) is specific: `git log` and `git diff-index` return no output or specific error messages. The tool must recognize this state and handle it gracefully. A mocked git output as empty might pass when the real tool crashes because git's actual error behavior on a no-commit repository differs from what a simple mock would return. Only initializing a real git repository with no commits will trigger the true edge case.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a new git repository with no commits (e.g., `git init`), run the tool on it, and verify that the tool produces an empty report (with no errors, no exceptions, and appropriate empty output format).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end correctness behavior: the tool must correctly invoke git, parse the output, aggregate counts across all change types, and format the report. Unit tests on isolated parsing logic might miss bugs in git invocation, multi-line parsing, or aggregation across different git commands. The boundary where this can fail is the integration between the git subprocess, output parsing, and counting logic. Only a real repository with known change history allows verification that counts are accurate.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test git repository with a precisely controlled change history (e.g., commit 3 files, delete 1, rename 2, modify 4), run the tool, and verify that the output report counts match exactly: 3 added, 1 deleted, 2 renamed, 4 modified.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Git's staging area is a real feature with specific behavior. The tool must produce identical output regardless of whether changes are staged (in the index) or unstaged (in the working tree). A mocked git output might not capture the subtle differences in how git reports staged vs. unstaged changes, or the code might incorrectly filter based on staging status. Only a real repository with actual staged and unstaged changes will expose logic errors in how the tool considers staging.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test git repository with uncommitted changes, run the tool once without staging (working tree only), then stage those changes and run the tool again, verifying that both runs produce identical output reports.
